title: Elliptical orbits: Kepler's laws fall out of the inverse-square law
scenario: A real orbit is not a circle but an *ellipse* with the planet at one focus ($\mu = GM = 4\times10^{14}$ m³/s², semi-major axis $a = 1.6\times10^{7}$ m). The satellite races through its closest point (perihelion) and crawls at its farthest (aphelion). There is no algebra formula for where it is at time $t$ — so the path is integrated numerically from $\ddot{\mathbf r}=-\mu\mathbf r/r^3$, and SymPy checks it conserves energy and angular momentum. Slide the eccentricity from a circle to a long ellipse: the shape changes completely, but because every orbit shares the same semi-major axis, they all take the *same time* to go around — Kepler's third law.

assumptions.claim: Two-body problem: a point (or spherical) planet far more massive than the satellite, whose mass cancels; no other bodies, no drag.
All orbits share the semi-major axis $a$ (so the same period), and stay above the planet's surface (perihelion $r_p = a(1-e) \ge 8\times10^6$ m even at $e=0.5$).

steps.label: Where the algebra-based course stops
steps.latex: v=\sqrt{GM/R}\quad\text{— only for a circle; no algebra formula for an ellipse's motion}
steps.prose: Centripetal balance handles a circle, but a real orbit is an *ellipse* with the body at one focus, speeding up near perihelion and slowing at aphelion. There is no algebra formula for where the planet is at time $t$ — the speed and radius change continuously.

steps.label: Solve the central-force ODE numerically
steps.prose: The same inverse-square law, but now the radius varies, so there is no elementary time-parameterisation (Kepler's equation is transcendental). Step the position and velocity forward with RK4 from perihelion. The producer checks the result conserves energy and angular momentum and that the orbit closes — verification, for a numerical solution.

steps.label: Kepler's first two laws are conservation laws
steps.prose: Energy conservation fixes the orbit's size (the semi-major axis $a$); angular-momentum conservation is **Kepler's second law** — the line from the body to the planet sweeps equal areas in equal times, so the planet races through perihelion and dawdles at aphelion. The closed curve is an ellipse with the body at one focus (**Kepler's first law**).

steps.label: Same semi-major axis, same period (Kepler's third law)
steps.prose: Slide the eccentricity: the orbit stretches from a circle to a long thin ellipse, but because every one shares the semi-major axis $a$, they all take the *same time* to go around. The period depends on $a$ alone — Kepler's third law, the same $T^2\propto a^3$ the circular lesson derived, now for any shape.

proof.heading: No closed form for the motion exists — so the ellipse is integrated numerically and machine-verified against the conservation laws (Kepler's three laws fall out).
proof.detail: integrate $\ddot{\mathbf r}=-\mu\mathbf r/r^3$ by RK4; check energy $\tfrac12 v^2-\mu/r$ and angular momentum $x\dot y-y\dot x$ are conserved; check the orbit closes; same $a\Rightarrow$ same $T$

checks.claim: The specific energy $\tfrac12 v^2-\mu/r=-\mu/2a$ is conserved along the orbit (max relative drift $4.7e-13$).
The angular momentum $x\dot y-y\dot x$ is conserved (max relative drift $1.1e-14$) — Kepler's second law: the line to the body sweeps equal areas in equal times.
After one period the path returns to its start (gap $<7.5e-12\,a$) — a closed ellipse (Kepler's first law).
Every orbit shares the semi-major axis $a$, so all have the same period $T=2\pi\sqrt{a^3/\mu}$ — Kepler's third law depends on $a$, not the shape.

frames.label: e = 0.2: ellipse, perihelion 12.80×10⁶ m, aphelion 19.20×10⁶ m
e = 0.4: ellipse, perihelion 9.60×10⁶ m, aphelion 22.40×10⁶ m
e = 0.5: ellipse, perihelion 8.00×10⁶ m, aphelion 24.00×10⁶ m

misconception.claim: A planet moves at constant speed around its orbit, and a more eccentric orbit takes longer to complete.
misconception.refuted_by: Neither is true. By **Kepler's second law** (angular momentum is conserved), the planet sweeps equal areas in equal times — so it moves *fastest* at perihelion and *slowest* at aphelion; at $e=0.5$ it is three times faster at its closest point than its farthest. And by **Kepler's third law** the period depends only on the semi-major axis $a$, not the eccentricity: a circle and a long thin ellipse with the same $a$ take *exactly the same time* to go around. Slide the eccentricity and watch the shape stretch while the period stays put.

practice.prompt: A satellite is on an ellipse of semi-major axis $a = 16000$ km around a body with $\mu = 4\times10^{14}$ m$^3$/s$^2$. What is its orbital period?

choices.misconception: Used the perihelion distance instead of the semi-major axis. The period depends on $a$ (the average of perihelion and aphelion): $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^3/\mu}$.
